citation: Patel NK, Hanna A, Johns WL, Dhanota AS, Dodson CC, Pepe M. Incidence, Management, and Outcomes following Elbow Ulnar Collateral Ligament Injuries in Non-Quarterback Professional Football Players. JSES International. 2025;doi:10.1016/j.jseint.2025.04.010.
abstract: This retrospective descriptive epidemiology study examined ulnar collateral ligament (UCL) injuries in non-quarterback NFL players using the NFL Injury Surveillance System (NFLISS) from 2015 to 2022. A total of 268 isolated UCL injuries were identified across the 7-year period, averaging 33.5±4.2 per year. Injury severity was graded I-III based on clinical examination and MRI findings, with management type and time to return to play (RTP) recorded. Offensive and defensive linemen accounted for 56% of all injuries, and 98.5% of players were treated non-operatively. Significant differences in time missed were found between Grade I (1.1 days) and Grade II (12.4 days) injuries (p<0.05) and between Grade I and Grade III (26.3 days) injuries (p<0.05).
tldr: UCL injuries in non-quarterback NFL players are predominantly mild Grade I sprains affecting linemen, and the vast majority resolve with non-operative treatment without any time lost. However, Grade II and III injuries result in significantly longer absences — up to 26 days on average — so grading severity early helps staff set realistic return-to-play timelines and counsel players appropriately.
methods: Retrospective review of the NFLISS database from 2015–2022 for non-quarterback players with isolated UCL injuries. Injuries were graded I–III based on laxity on clinical examination or MRI. Player demographics, position, injury mechanism (contact vs. non-contact), timing (preseason/regular season/postseason), setting (practice vs. game), treatment type (operative vs. non-operative), and days missed were extracted. T-tests and Mann-Whitney U tests compared time missed by injury grade; significance set at p<0.05.
findings: 268 total isolated UCL injuries identified (33.5±4.2/year). Offensive and defensive linemen accounted for 56% (150/268) of injuries; offensive linemen alone were 33.5%. Grade distribution: 73% Grade I, 16.8% Grade II, 10.1% Grade III. 72% of players missed no time; of those who did, mean time missed was 5.4±24.2 days. Grade I vs. Grade II time missed: 1.1 vs. 12.4 days (p<0.05); Grade I vs. Grade III: 1.1 vs. 26.3 days (p<0.05). Only 4 players (1.5%) required surgery (3 linebackers, 1 special teams); surgical RTP averaged 4.7 months. All injuries occurred via contact mechanism.
limitations: Retrospective nature and reliance on NFLISS reporting by individual team medical staffs may underrepresent true incidence. Time missed may be confounded by injury timing within the season. MRI details (tear type, location, extent) were unavailable. Non-operative treatment specifics (bracing, PRP, duration) were not recorded. Patient-reported outcome data and return-to-performance measures are unavailable through NFLISS. Surgical indications, techniques, and long-term follow-up are unknown.
practicalImplications: Applied sport science staff should expect that most UCL elbow injuries in non-QB players will not result in missed time and can be managed non-operatively. Injury severity grading at the time of diagnosis is critical for setting RTP expectations: Grade I injuries typically allow same-week return, while Grade II injuries average nearly 2 weeks and Grade III injuries average nearly 4 weeks. Linemen and linebackers are highest-risk positions and should be monitored closely during contact-heavy blocks and drills.
athleteDev: Given that linemen sustain over half of all UCL injuries in the NFL through valgus contact forces at the elbow during blocking, position-specific elbow stability and load tolerance training should be integrated into athlete development programs. Strengthening the medial elbow stabilizers and teaching blocking mechanics that minimize valgus stress may reduce incidence, particularly for offensive linemen who represent the single most frequently injured position group.
rtp: UCL injury grade is the primary driver of RTP timeline in non-QB NFL players. Grade I sprains: near-zero time loss expected, return same week is routine. Grade II sprains: expect approximately 12 days absence, non-operative management. Grade III sprains: expect approximately 26 days absence, still typically non-operative. Surgical cases: RTP averaged 4.7 months, potentially reflecting repair vs. reconstruction differences. RTP is defined as full unrestricted practice or game participation.

citation: Cairns MA, Hasty EK, Herzog MM, Ostrum RF, Kerr ZY. Incidence, Severity, and Time Loss Associated With Collegiate Football Fractures, 2004-2005 to 2013-2014. Am J Sports Med. 2018;doi:10.1177/0363546517749914.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study analyzed fracture data from NCAA football programs reported to the NCAA Injury Surveillance Program (NCAA-ISP) during the 2004-2005 through 2013-2014 academic years. A total of 986 fractures were identified, representing 4.4% of all injuries during this period and an overall rate of 0.32 per 1000 athlete-exposures. Competition fractures occurred at 10.56 times the rate of practice fractures. The most common fracture sites were the hand/fingers (34.6%) and fibula (17.2%), while 62.5% of all fractures resulted in more than 21 days of time loss. Overall, 34.4% of fractures required surgical intervention, and surgically managed fractures were significantly more likely to result in extended time loss.
tldr: Fractures are relatively uncommon in collegiate football (4.4% of all injuries) but carry significant consequences — nearly two-thirds result in more than 3 weeks out of participation, and one-third require surgery. Hand and fibula fractures are most common, and surgically treated fractures are far more likely to cause extended time loss. Staff should anticipate prolonged absences and surgical referrals for lower extremity fractures in particular.
methods: Retrospective analysis of NCAA-ISP data from academic years 2004-2005 through 2013-2014, limited to injuries with a reported diagnosis of fracture. Data were collected electronically from an average of 43 schools per year by certified athletic trainers. Fracture rates per 1000 athlete-exposures (AEs), injury rate ratios (IRRs), injury proportion ratios (IPRs), and 95% CIs were calculated. Time loss was categorized as 1-6 days, 7-21 days, or >21 days. Statistical significance defined as 95% CI excluding 1.0.
findings: 986 total fractures reported; 50.7% in competition, 49.3% in practice. Competition fracture rate was 10.56x practice rate (1.80 vs 0.17 per 1000 AEs). Most common fractures: hand/fingers 34.6%, fibula 17.2%, foot 12.0%, shoulder 7.5%, arm/elbow 6.9%. 62.5% of all fractures resulted in >21 days time loss. 34.4% required surgery; surgically managed fractures were more likely to result in >21 days lost (85.0% vs 50.7%; IPR=1.68). 6.3% were recurrent; recurrent hand/finger fractures were more likely to require surgery (66.7% vs 27.2%; IPR=2.45). Lower leg fractures: 94.4% resulted in >21 days time loss, 53.8% required surgery.
limitations: Reliance on athletic trainer reporting introduces potential inaccuracy. No player position data available, which could influence surgery incidence and time loss. Time loss is not further stratified beyond 21 days. Timing of injury within the season may affect reported time loss. Surgical indications and specific procedures are not captured. Potential underreporting of surgeries performed after completion of collegiate career.
practicalImplications: Collegiate football medical and sport science staff should treat any lower extremity fracture as a presumptive multi-week absence requiring surgical evaluation. Hand and finger fractures, the most common type, have highly variable outcomes. Prevention initiatives targeting fracture reduction — particularly in competition settings where the rate is 10x that of practice — are warranted. Fifth metatarsal fractures have a notably high recurrence rate and surgical need, requiring cautious RTP protocols.
athleteDev: The 10-fold higher fracture rate in competition versus practice underscores the importance of progressive physical preparation and sport-specific loading during preseason. Hand and finger fractures at 34.6% of all fractures suggest that linemen and skill players exposed to high contact forces at the extremities should have protective equipment regularly evaluated. Lower leg fracture prevention programs and bone stress monitoring in high-load athletes may reduce season-ending injuries.
rtp: Fracture RTP in collegiate football is highly site-dependent. Hand/finger fractures may allow return within 1-6 days in some cases (~35%), but thumb and metacarpal fractures more often require 3+ weeks. Shoulder and arm/elbow fractures almost universally result in >21 days loss. Lower leg, ankle, and foot fractures causing >21 days loss in 85-95% of cases should receive extended RTP protocols. Recurrent fifth metatarsal fractures carry heightened surgical risk and demand careful graduated RTP.

citation: Posmanick B, Pinheiro R, Ameis D, Fay S. Income Taxes and Firm Competitiveness: A Case Study from the National Football League. International Journal of Financial Studies. 2025;13(4):212.
abstract: This economics study uses NFL teams from 1984 to 2000 to estimate the causal effect of state income taxes on team performance before and after implementation of the NFL salary cap in 1994. A difference-in-differences model was employed with college football teams as the control group, as college players during this era received no compensation and thus were not affected by tax rates. The Simple Rating System (SRS) served as the primary measure of team quality. Results show that higher state income taxes significantly reduced team quality as measured by SRS, with the effect substantially amplified after the salary cap was introduced. Decision tree analysis confirmed that teams in states with tax rates above approximately 7% shifted from being more likely to be above average (pre-cap) to more likely to be below average (post-cap).
tldr: NFL teams in high-income-tax states face a measurable competitive disadvantage — particularly after the 1994 salary cap — because player wages must be grossed up to offset tax burdens, effectively reducing the real value of the salary cap. For football organizations in high-tax states, this supports a roster-building strategy favoring the draft and trades over free agency, and highlights the structural economic challenge that state tax policy creates for competitive balance.
methods: Difference-in-differences (DID) regression using NFL (treatment) and college football FBS Division 1 (control) teams from 1984–2000. Salary cap implementation in 1994 served as the treatment. Dependent variables: team winning percentage and SRS. Key independent variables: top state income tax bracket rate, salary cap indicator (NFL teams post-1994), and their interaction term. Control variables: median household income, state population, strength of schedule, and team fixed effects. Three control groups tested: all FBS college teams, power conference teams only, and hand-matched state-level pairs. Decision tree analysis using Gini impurity minimization classified teams as above/below average SRS before and after the cap.
findings: Full sample DID: tax rate negatively associated with SRS (coefficient -31.45, p<0.1); Tax × Salary Cap interaction: -35.03 (p<0.1). Power conference sample: tax effect -54.95 (p<0.01), interaction -36.45 (p<0.1). Hand-matched sample: tax effect -51.27 (p<0.01), interaction -36.46 (p<0.05). Pre-cap decision tree: teams with tax rate ≥7.2% were above average 60% of the time. Post-cap decision tree: teams with tax rate ≥7.0% were above average only 36% of the time; teams with tax rate <7.0% were above average 58% of the time.
limitations: Study period limited to 1984–2000; may not reflect modern salary cap era. Parallel trends assumption cannot be directly tested post-treatment. Only state-level top marginal tax rates used; local city taxes and effective rates not incorporated. Individual player-level compensation data not used. The 'good-team discount' is acknowledged but not modeled.
practicalImplications: NFL front offices in high-tax states should factor state income tax rates into free agency negotiations, as teams may need to pay approximately 9.5% more in gross wages to attract equivalent talent. Roster-building strategy for high-tax teams should skew toward the draft and internal development rather than free agency bidding. Sport science staff in high-tax markets should be aware that effective player acquisition budgets may be constrained relative to low-tax competitors.
athleteDev: While not directly focused on athlete physical development, this study has relevance for team resource allocation: in high-tax states, salary cap efficiency demands greater return on investment from drafted and developed players. Player development programs in such organizations may face implicit pressure to maximize contributions from lower-cost, internally developed athletes, potentially increasing the relative value of robust sport science and athletic development infrastructure.
rtp: This paper does not address return-to-play protocols or injury management. It is an economics study focused on team competitiveness and fiscal policy. No RTP-relevant data or findings are present.

citation: Bi AS, Azam MT, Butler JJ, Alaia MJ, Jazrawi LM, Gonzalez-Lomas G, Kennedy JG. Increased incidence of acute Achilles tendon ruptures in the peri-pandemic COVID era with parallels to the 2021-22 NFL season. Knee Surgery, Sports Traumatology, Arthroscopy. 2023;doi:10.1007/s00167-023-07309-y.
abstract: This retrospective study investigated rates of acute Achilles tendon ruptures (AATRs) at a single urban academic institution in New York from 2017 to 2021, with a parallel analysis of NFL injured reserve data from the 2019-20 through 2021-22 regular seasons. A total of 1,431 AATRs were identified during the 5-year period, with 588 undergoing primary surgical repair. AATR incidence declined 7.5% from 2019 to 2020 during COVID quarantine, then rebounded 8.9% from 2020 to 2021 — exceeding pre-pandemic rates. Within the NFL, AATRs resulting in injured reserve designations increased annually from 11 (2019-20) to 17 (2020-21) to 24 (2021-22), mirroring the pattern seen in recreational athletes.
tldr: Extended periods of physical inactivity — whether from COVID quarantine or a canceled preseason — appear to prime the Achilles tendon for rupture upon return to activity. NFL data from 2019-2022 shows a steady and concerning rise in Achilles tendon injuries on injured reserve, with the 2021-22 season showing the highest count in recent history. Sport science staff should implement structured, progressive Achilles loading programs following any prolonged layoff to reduce rupture risk when athletes return to full competition.
methods: Retrospective review at NYU Langone Health using CPT codes for Achilles repair (27650, 27652, 27654) from January 2017 to December 2021. Non-operative AATRs identified via ICD-10 codes. Acute primary repairs only included; augmentations, reconstructions, and tendon transfers excluded. Total sports surgery volume used as denominator control. NFL analysis: retrospective review of publicly available injury reports for players placed on injured reserve due to Achilles injury in 2019-20, 2020-21, and 2021-22 regular seasons. Chi-square analysis conducted to compare proportions across years.
findings: Total AATRs: 1,431 over 5 years (2017: n=288, 2018: n=261, 2019: n=291, 2020: n=196, 2021: n=395). Surgical repairs: 2017: 124, 2018: 110, 2019: 130, 2020: 86, 2021: 138. 2021 had significantly higher proportion of Achilles repairs vs. 2020 (p=0.008), 2018 (p=0.007), 2017 (p=0.03), and 2017-2020 combined (p=0.009), but not vs. 2019. NFL IR-level AATRs rose from 11 (2019-20) to 17 (2020-21) to 24 (2021-22), a 118% increase over 3 seasons. Cohort: 75.7% male, avg age 43.2±14.4 years, avg BMI 28.6.
limitations: Single-institution study at an urban academic center; may not reflect national trends. CPT code capture only estimates AATR rate. Patient migration out of NYC during 2020 may have artificially lowered case counts. Non-operative management trends during COVID may have shifted cases. NFL data prior to 2019-20 relied on previously published studies. Only IR-designation injuries captured in NFL analysis.
practicalImplications: Sport science and medical staff should treat any prolonged period of inactivity as a high-risk window for Achilles tendon injury upon return. Progressive tendon loading protocols should be implemented before returning to full practice. Passive stretching has limited protective value; balance and proprioception training programs have demonstrated benefit. Tendon adaptation requires time, and rapid workload spikes following deconditioning are the primary danger.
athleteDev: Achilles tendon health should be a monitored component of return-to-activity programming after any extended layoff. Athletes with longer periods of inactivity (8+ weeks) may require dedicated Achilles-specific progressive loading phases — including eccentric protocols, isometrics, and plyometric progressions — before returning to full practice demands.
rtp: Following Achilles tendon rupture in NFL players, IR designation was virtually universal, indicating season-ending or multi-month absences. For players returning from quarantine or canceled preseason, graduated return protocols should include Achilles loading benchmarks before clearance for full-contact practice. The parallel between recreational athlete AATR increases post-COVID and NFL IR spikes in 2021-22 provides strong support for mandatory progressive Achilles preparation protocols after any prolonged layoff.

citation: Sclafani S, Frane N, Humphrey TJ, Miceli J, Trasolini R. Increased regular season soft tissue injury rates in National Football League players may be associated with the canceled 2020 NFL preseason due to COVID-19. Cureus. 2022;14(5):e24674.
abstract: This retrospective study compared soft tissue injury rates in the NFL's 2020-2021 regular season (canceled preseason) versus the 2018-2019 regular season (normal preseason) using publicly available injury report data. Eight soft tissue injury categories were analyzed: hamstring, groin, calf, quadriceps, thigh, knee-ACL, pectoral, and Achilles. Total injuries in 2020-2021 (n=2086) were substantially higher than in 2018-2019 (n=1370), with overall injury rates per 1000 athletic exposures of 88.57 vs. 58.17 (p<0.001). Hamstring, groin, calf, quadriceps, and thigh injuries were all significantly elevated in 2020-2021, while ACL, pectoral, and Achilles injuries did not reach statistical significance, possibly due to lower event rates or injury mechanism differences.
tldr: The canceled 2020 NFL preseason due to COVID-19 was associated with a 52% increase in total soft tissue injuries during the regular season, with hamstring, groin, calf, quad, and thigh injuries each significantly elevated. This parallels findings from the 2011 NFL lockout and strongly supports the protective role of preseason and gradual workload progression. For applied sport science staff, this is powerful evidence that any abrupt increase in training load following a deconditioning period dramatically elevates soft tissue injury risk.
methods: Retrospective review of publicly available NFL injury reports (NFL.com) for the 2020-2021 and 2018-2019 regular seasons. Injury data points defined by unique anatomic location and game status per weekly report. Injury rates calculated per 1000 athletic exposures (23,552 AEs/season: 46 active players × 2 teams × 256 games). Chi-square tests for categorical comparisons; Student's t-test for continuous variables. Eight soft tissue injury categories analyzed; all preseason, postseason injuries and illness designations excluded.
findings: Total injuries: 2018-19=1,370; 2020-21=2,086. Overall rate/1000 AEs: 58.17 vs 88.57 (p<0.001). Significantly higher in 2020-21: hamstring (9.98 vs 5.31, p=0.043), groin (5.56 vs 2.46, p=0.007), calf (4.08 vs 1.61, p=0.006), quadriceps (2.00 vs 0.72, p=0.030), thigh (1.23 vs 0.30, p=0.012). Non-significant: knee-ACL (p=0.251), pectoral (p=0.165), Achilles (p=0.214, identical 0.72 rate both seasons). Average duration of hamstring, groin, quadriceps, and thigh injuries was significantly shorter in 2020-21. No significant age differences between seasons.
limitations: Data integrity depends on accurate team-level injury reporting. Causality cannot be definitively established. Individual-level risk factor analysis not possible from population database. Variation in rest periods between games not captured. Team-specific return-to-play protocol differences not accounted for. Only 2 comparison seasons used. Cannot account for potential rule enforcement or gameplay changes.
practicalImplications: Strong empirical support for the protective role of NFL preseason training on regular season soft tissue injury rates. Sport science staff should advocate for maintaining preseason training continuity as the primary strategy to mitigate muscle strain injuries. Injury surveillance should be heightened in early weeks of any season following abbreviated preparation. Rapid workload increases from a low chronic baseline (acute:chronic workload imbalance) drive hamstring, groin, calf, and thigh injury risk.
athleteDev: Gradual workload progression is a foundational injury prevention principle for NFL athletes. Off-season programming that maintains a chronic training base — even during disruptions — is protective against early-season soft tissue breakdown. Hamstring eccentric strength, posterior chain flexibility, and sport-specific movement patterns are particularly vulnerable following deconditioning. Development programs should incorporate workload monitoring tools (acute:chronic ratios) to prevent dangerous spikes.
rtp: Players returning from extended inactivity are at significantly elevated risk for hamstring, groin, calf, quadriceps, and thigh injuries once full practice and game demands resume. RTP protocols following deconditioning periods should include structured tissue loading phases before full-contact clearance, with particular attention to hamstring eccentric strength deficits and posterior chain flexibility. The shorter average injury duration in 2020-21 suggests milder injuries on average, but the sheer volume increase still poses significant roster management challenges.
